agent1_raw: The AUROC and recall comparisons in this memo are built on shifting ground. The LSTM's published numbers come from a 24-hour severe-sepsis deterioration task; the transformer's numbers come from a 12-hour vasopressor-escalation task. Different endpoints produce different scores even for the same underlying model. The lab timestamping also changed — charted timestamps versus specimen collection time — which affects which features appear before the prediction horizon. The cohort unit changed from individual admissions to merged cross-transfer episodes. And encounter-level random splitting in a five-year multi-admission ICU dataset is not appropriate when the same patients appear repeatedly. The transformer could still be superior under a fair comparison, but this memo does not demonstrate that.
agent2_raw: Each paragraph of this memo introduces a change that was not held constant: the prediction endpoint changed, the lab timestamp convention changed, the cohort construction rule changed, and the split policy changed. When all four change simultaneously, the benchmark's AUROC and recall comparison reflects the combination of model architecture and evaluation design choices, not model quality alone. The external validation result (Hospital B, 1126 episodes, prespecified power) is more reliable because it was applied to the transformer only — not a cross-model comparison — and shows performance in a new site.
agent3_raw: The benchmark silently changes four evaluation conditions alongside the model architecture: prediction endpoint, timestamp policy, cohort construction, and split grain. These are all documented in the memo but no paragraph directly states that they make the comparison non-comparable. The most important is the endpoint change: 24-hour severe-sepsis screening versus 12-hour vasopressor onset are genuinely different clinical tasks, and the difficulty difference alone could account for part of the AUROC gap. A patient-level chronological split on the harmonized cohort would provide a clean baseline for the transformer before and after controlling for the other changes.
synthesis_raw: All three agents converge on empirical_test_agreed. The four benchmark design mismatches are identified consistently. None incorrectly challenges lactate/vasopressor feature use or the prespecified external cohort size. All support a harmonized rerun before promotion. ETD conditions specified: shared cohort, one endpoint, one timestamp policy, patient-level chronological split.
all_issues_raised: Endpoint changed: LSTM on 24h severe-sepsis, transformer on 12h vasopressor onset
Timestamp policy changed from EHR-charted to specimen collection time
Encounter-level random split creates patient leakage in multi-admission ICU dataset
Cohort changed from individual admissions to merged cross-transfer episodes

empirical_test.condition: Rebuild single cohort with shared endpoint, timestamp policy, cohort construction rule, and patient-level chronological split; rerun both models
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: AUROC edge shrinks below 0.01 and recall advantage disappears on harmonized internal and Hospital B cohorts
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer still leads by at least 0.02 AUROC and 5+ recall points at same alert rate on both hospitals
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Modest internal AUROC gain persists but recall advantage disappears, or gains are inconsistent across hospitals
